model_narrative: We manage replenishment for a collection of items. Each item must be assigned exactly one replenishment schedule chosen from a fixed menu of candidate schedules, and that choice fixes how often the item is ordered per unit time. Every item-schedule pairing carries a known cost. We want to pick one schedule for each item so that the total cost across all items is as small as possible, while respecting an overall limit on how much ordering activity we can do.

sets.name: nn
sets.doc: the items to be stocked, each replenished independently

sets.name: mm
sets.doc: the candidate replenishment schedules; each member corresponds to one allowed value for how many orders an item is placed on per unit time

params.name: N
params.doc: the cap on the total number of replenishments allowed across all items

params.name: Y
params.doc: the number of orders per unit time associated with each candidate schedule; choosing schedule mm for an item sets that item's order frequency to this value

params.name: Dv
params.doc: for each item, its demand rate multiplied by its unit cost

params.name: CostZ
params.doc: the cost incurred for item nn if it is assigned candidate schedule mm; precomputed as a fixed coefficient for each item-schedule pair

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the chosen number of orders per unit time for each item; bounded below by the smallest candidate order frequency and above by the largest

vars.name: z
vars.doc: equals 1 if item nn is assigned candidate schedule mm, and 0 otherwise

vars.name: obj
vars.doc: the total cost accumulated over all chosen item-schedule assignments

constraints.name: obj
constraints.intent: The total cost being minimized is built up from the chosen assignments. Add up, across every item and every candidate schedule, the cost of pairing that item with that schedule whenever the item is actually assigned to it, and set the total cost equal to that sum.

constraints.name: x[nn]
constraints.intent: There is an overall ceiling on ordering activity. Adding up the chosen order frequency across all items, the total must not exceed three times the allowed limit on replenishments.

constraints.name: z[nn,
constraints.intent: Each item's order frequency follows from the schedule it is assigned. For every item, its chosen number of orders per unit time equals the order frequency of whichever candidate schedule it is assigned to.

constraints.name: z[nn,
constraints.intent: Every item must be assigned exactly one replenishment schedule. For each item, exactly one of the candidate schedules is selected and the rest are not.

query: Our planners are weighing a rule that keeps at most 20 SKUs parked on the rock-bottom reorder schedule — they think too many are stuck at the floor. Layer that ceiling in and tell me what total cost comes out to.
expected_pyomo: model.cap_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.z[n, 'i1'] for n in model.nn) <= 20)

query: There's a two-part proposal on the table for our six top-value SKUs: hold their combined reorder frequency to no more than 90 orders a period, and at the same time make sure item 22 gets replenished at least as often as item 30. We're evaluating the package — plug both in together and tell me where total inventory cost lands.
expected_pyomo:
model.budget_top = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[n] for n in ['n43', 'n44', 'n45', 'n46', 'n47', 'n48']) <= 90)
model.pace_n22 = Constraint(expr=model.x['n22'] >= model.x['n30'])

query: There's a proposal to push more service into our six highest-value SKUs as a group: their combined reorder frequency across the period should add up to at least 130 orders. Layer that floor in and walk me through what total inventory cost the optimizer returns.

query: Procurement is pitching a minimum service level: every SKU should be reordered at least six times a period, so nothing sits on the bare-minimum schedule. Fold that in and tell me the total cost it implies.
expected_pyomo: model.min_service = Constraint(expr=sum(model.z[n, 'i1'] for n in model.nn) <= 0)

query: Item 27 carries real value, yet it's stranded on the rock-bottom reorder schedule while item 37 gets replenished far more often. Shouldn't a SKU like 27 be reordered at least as frequently as item 37 rather than sitting at the floor? What's the model seeing that keeps it from making that move?

query: A whole cluster of our mid-to-upper-value SKUs — items 20 through 27 — are all parked on the rock-bottom reorder schedule. None of these is a throwaway item; wouldn't it be obvious that not a single one of them should be left on the bare-minimum cadence? What's the tradeoff pulling the plan toward leaving them all at the floor?

query: Items 47 and 48 are our two single most valuable SKUs, yet the plan parks both of them on the very same modest i5 cadence. Surely our two crown-jewel items shouldn't both be stuck at that identical schedule — couldn't at least one of them be pushed onto a more aggressive cadence? What's keeping the plan from separating those two?
